notes: Arrival day in Beijing. Land at PEK, clear customs, and take the Airport Express to Dongzhimen (¥25, 20 min). Check into the hutong inn and drop luggage. Evening walk through Nanluoguxiang — grab jianbing (savoury crepes) from a street cart and explore the lantern-lit alley. Early night; jet lag is real.
tagline: Touch down and wander the hutongs

notes: Full day at the Forbidden City and Tiananmen. Be at the South Gate by 8 am to beat the crowds — book tickets in advance online (¥60 pp). Walk the full north–south axis, don't miss the Clock Exhibition Hall. Afternoon: stroll through Jingshan Park for the classic rooftop view back over the golden rooftops. Dinner at Dadong Roast Duck — order the crispy suckling duck, not just the Peking duck, if they have it.
tagline: Forbidden City, Jingshan Park & Peking duck

notes: Great Wall day. Take a private taxi to Mutianyu (less crowded than Badaling, more dramatic than Simatai for a day trip). Cable car up, toboggan run down — genuinely fun. The wall here dates to the Ming dynasty; walk east from the cable car for views and almost no people. Pack lunch or eat at the village below. Afternoon back in the hutongs — rent bikes and cycle the Second Ring Road canal path.
tagline: Great Wall hike and toboggan descent

notes: Temple circuit and departure prep. Morning at the Temple of Heaven (arrive early for the extraordinary sight of elderly Beijingers practising tai chi, ballroom dancing and opera in the park). Afternoon: Summer Palace for the Long Corridor and Kunming Lake boat ride. Evening at Wangfujing night market — try stinky tofu if you dare. Pack tonight; early train to Xi'an tomorrow.
tagline: Temples, Summer Palace & night market

notes: Morning high-speed train Beijing to Xi'an (about 4.5 hrs, G-train, book in advance). Check in and walk the Muslim Quarter immediately — it comes alive in the afternoon. The stretch from the Drum Tower down to the Great Mosque is one of China's great street food corridors: roujiamo (spiced lamb in flatbread), biangbiang noodles, and pomegranate juice squeezed to order. Buy a night ticket to climb the city walls at sunset.
tagline: Train to Xi'an and Muslim Quarter feast

notes: Terracotta Army day. Leave by 8 am via tourist bus from the train station (Line 5, ¥7). Pit 1 is overwhelming in scale; Pit 3 is smaller but shows the command structure; the museum building housing the bronze chariots is unmissable. Hire an English-speaking guide at the entrance — they double the experience. Return by 3 pm and spend the evening cycling the full perimeter of the ancient city walls (13 km, about 1.5 hrs). Dinner: Defachang restaurant for dumpling banquet.
tagline: Terracotta Army and cycling the city walls

notes: Tang dynasty culture day. Morning at the Big Wild Goose Pagoda (climb all seven storeys for city views) and the adjacent Tang Paradise garden. Afternoon at the Shaanxi History Museum — one of China's finest, free with passport, arrive early as queues form fast. See the Tang dynasty gold and silver collection on the top floor. Evening Silk Road souvenir shopping in the Shuyuanmen calligraphy street.
tagline: Pagoda, history museum & Silk Road shopping

notes: Morning train or flight to Chengdu (high-speed train ~3.5 hrs). Go straight to the Giant Panda Breeding Research Base — arrive before 10 am to see pandas eating bamboo (the rest of the day they sleep). Book the volunteer "keeper for a day" session if you want a once-in-a-lifetime experience. Afternoon: check in and explore the Yulin neighbourhood's teahouses and mahjong parlours. Dinner: Du Jiangyan mapo tofu on Yulin South Road.
tagline: Pandas in the morning, tea houses in the afternoon

notes: Deep dive into Sichuan food culture. Morning: cooking class at a local school — learn mapo tofu, kung pao chicken and dan dan noodles from scratch. Lunch is the class output. Afternoon: Jinli Ancient Street for atmosphere and snacks (rabbit head, three-pepper skewers, osmanthus cake). Evening: book a hot-pot dinner at Haidilao or a local neighbourhood spot — the communal pot experience with numbing mala broth is unmissable. Order the sesame oil dipping sauce.
tagline: Cooking class and a mala hot-pot night

notes: Day trip to Leshan Giant Buddha. Take the early bus (2 hrs each way). The 71 m seated Buddha carved into the cliff face at the confluence of three rivers is the largest stone Buddha on earth — take the boat for the full perspective, then walk down the cliff staircase for the close-up. On the way back, stop at the Sanxingdui Museum (Bronze Age civilisation, genuinely mysterious artefacts, very underrated). Evening: Sichuan opera face-changing show at the Shufeng Yayun theatre.
tagline: Leshan Giant Buddha and Sichuan opera

notes: Train or bus to Chongqing (1.5 hrs from Chengdu). Check in and immediately head up to the Hongya Cave complex at dusk — this 11-storey building cascading down the cliff above the Jialing River is spectacular at golden hour. Dinner in one of the riverside restaurants inside the cave. After dinner, take the Yangtze River cable car across to the South Bank and walk the Nanbin Lu promenade for the full neon skyline view.
tagline: Arrive Chongqing and Hongya Cave at dusk

notes: Chongqing city exploration. Morning: hike up to the Eling Park pavilion for the best panoramic view of the Yangtze–Jialing confluence. Then descend to Ciqikou Ancient Town — a Ming-dynasty river port that has survived intact, perfect for exploring and buying Sichuan pickles and spices. Afternoon: the Three Gorges Museum on People's Square (free, covers the history and archaeology displaced by the Three Gorges Dam). Evening: Chongqing hot-pot dinner — order the "yuan-yang" half-and-half pot if anyone in the group is heat-sensitive.
tagline: River views, ancient town & hot-pot

notes: Travel day to Zhangjiajie (flight or train from Chongqing, ~2 hrs). Check in and take a gentle orientation hike in the national park in the late afternoon before the light fades — the Yuanjiajie platform area gives the classic "Avatar" columns view. Dinner at the lodge; early night because tomorrow is a full park day.
tagline: Arrive and first glimpse of Avatar columns

notes: Full day in Zhangjiajie National Forest Park. Start at sunrise on the Yuanjiajie plateau (most dramatic lighting on the columns). Walk the cliff-hanging Tianzi Mountain trails. Take the Bailong Elevator (the world's tallest outdoor elevator, 330 m, glass-sided) down through the cliff face — genuinely terrifying. Afternoon: explore the Jinbian Stream canyon trail, a 7 km riverside walk through the densest concentration of pillars. Pack snacks; food stalls are limited inside the park.
tagline: Full park day — pillars, cliffs & glass elevator

notes: Tianmen Mountain and glass bridge day. Morning: Tianmen Mountain cable car (longest passenger ropeway in the world, 7.5 km). At the summit: Tianmen Cave natural arch, the Heaven's Gate stairs (999 steps — worth every one), and the cliff-hanging glass walkway. Afternoon: drive to the Zhangjiajie Grand Canyon glass bridge (book separately; the world's longest and highest glass-bottomed bridge). A genuinely dizzying experience over a 300 m drop.
tagline: Heaven's Gate stairs and glass bridge vertigo

notes: Travel to Guilin by high-speed train (~2.5 hrs). Check in at the Yangshuo guesthouse (most visiting is done from Yangshuo rather than Guilin city). Afternoon: rent bicycles and cycle the Yulong River route through rice paddies and karst hills — arguably the most beautiful cycling in China. Evening: catch the "Impression Liu Sanjie" outdoor river show directed by Zhang Yimou, performed against the karst backdrop at night on the Li River.
tagline: Arrive Guilin and cycle the Yulong River

notes: Li River cruise day. Board the cruise boat at Guilin pier (departs ~9 am, arrives Yangshuo ~1 pm). This 83 km stretch is the scenery printed on the Chinese ¥20 note — limestone peaks, river mist, cormorant fishermen. Disembark in Yangshuo and spend the afternoon exploring West Street and the surrounding villages. Try Yangshuo beer fish (local speciality) for lunch and Guilin rice noodles for a late breakfast on the boat.
tagline: Li River cruise through karst paradise

notes: Reed Flute Cave and Elephant Trunk Hill. Morning: Reed Flute Cave near Guilin city (3,000-year-old cave system with extraordinary stalactite formations, well-lit and easy to walk). Return to Yangshuo via Elephant Trunk Hill, an 800-year-old natural rock arch where the hill perfectly resembles an elephant drinking from the river. Afternoon free — optional rock climbing on the karst faces around Yangshuo (world-class routes for all levels, rental gear available). Last night dinner: splurge at a riverside restaurant in Yangshuo.
tagline: Caves, karst hills & farewell dinner

notes: Travel to Hong Kong (train to Guilin North station, then high-speed to Shenzhen, cross border on foot via Lo Wu, MTR into Hong Kong — or fly direct from Guilin Liangjiang Airport). Check in and immediately take the Star Ferry across the harbour to Kowloon — Hong Kong's most iconic experience costs HK$3.40. Walk the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront promenade at dusk. Dinner in the Temple Street Night Market — try egg waffles, curry fishballs and HK-style milk tea.
tagline: Star Ferry, harbour lights & night market

notes: Victoria Peak and Central exploration. Morning: ride the century-old Peak Tram to the summit — go early before cloud rolls in. The 360-degree view of the harbour, islands and mountains is the defining Hong Kong panorama. Walk back down via the tree-lined Lugard Road circuit. Afternoon: explore Central and Sheung Wan on foot — Hollywood Road antique shops, the Man Mo Temple, and PMQ creative market. Dim sum lunch at Lin Heung Tea House (old-school trolley service, arrive before 11 am).
tagline: Victoria Peak and dim sum in Central

notes: Last full day. Morning: Lantau Island — take the MTR to Tung Chung, then the Ngong Ping cable car to the Big Buddha (Tian Tan Buddha) and Po Lin Monastery for a vegetarian lunch. Afternoon back in the city: browse the Graham Street market, pick up last-minute souvenirs in the lanes around Hollywood Road. Evening: watch the Symphony of Lights laser show from the Tsim Sha Tsui promenade (8 pm nightly) — a fitting farewell to China. Celebratory group dinner at a Cantonese roast goose restaurant.
tagline: Big Buddha, laser show & roast goose farewell
